title: Dog Short Stride on Back Legs: Causes, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Noticed your dog taking shorter steps? Learn about rear leg stiffness, common causes like arthritis, and when you should consult a veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: A short stride in dogs is typically a sign of discomfort or joint stiffness that warrants a veterinary exam to rule out chronic conditions. If your dog suddenly cannot walk or is in extreme pain, seek emergency care immediately.

What does it mean if my dog has a short stride in their back legs?

  • A short stride, or stilted gait, occurs when a dog limits hind limb extension and flexion to protect a joint or muscle.
  • Instead of a fluid, swinging motion, the dog takes quick, choppy steps.
  • This behavior is commonly seen in dogs dealing with stiffness, inflammation, or structural changes in the hips, knees, or lower spine.

Is a short stride in dogs a veterinary emergency?

  • Low Urgency: If the onset is gradual and your dog is still eating, drinking, and walking, schedule a veterinary appointment within the week.
  • High Urgency: Seek immediate veterinary attention if the short stride is accompanied by a sudden inability to walk, dragging of the toes, or extreme vocalization.

How can a video or photo of my dog's gait help a veterinarian?

  • Visual evidence helps because adrenaline at the clinic can temporarily mask lameness or stiffness during an in-person exam.
  • Recording a video on a flat, non-slip surface at home allows the vet to analyze the gait in a relaxed environment.
  • This helps the vet pinpoint whether the issue originates from the hip, stifle, or hock, leading to a faster and more accurate diagnosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

A short-strided gait in the pelvic limbs can be indicative of several underlying conditions. In cases of acute idiopathic polyradiculoneuritis, the gait abnormality progresses rapidly (within 1-2 days) to flaccid tetraparesis or tetraplegia, and spinal cord reflexes are weak to absent. Cervical spondylomyelopathy can also manifest as a short-strided gait in the thoracic limbs, sometimes accompanied by a long, protracted stride in the pelvic limbs; more severe cases can involve paresis or paralysis of all limbs. When evaluating hindlimb lameness, dropping of the head during weight-bearing on the affected limb may be observed. Diagnostic evaluation should include palpation of the limbs (distal to proximal) to assess for swelling, pain, instability, crepitation, reduced range of motion, and muscle atrophy, as well as advanced imaging such as radiography, CT, or MRI.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1240)

faqs.question: Is my dog in pain if they are walking with a short stride?
faqs.answer: Yes, a change in gait is almost always a compensatory reaction to discomfort or physical restriction. Dogs are stoic and may not whine, but a shortened stride is a clear sign they are feeling stiff or sore.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog aspirin for their leg stiffness?
faqs.answer: You should never give human pain medications to dogs without a specific veterinary prescription, as many are toxic to canines. Your vet can provide safe, dog-specific anti-inflammatories.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of a short rear stride?
faqs.answer: Common causes include hip dysplasia, arthritis, cruciate ligament issues, or mild soft tissue strains. In older dogs, degenerative joint disease is the most frequent culprit.
